lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 9 - Attention, Final Projects and LLM Intro

unit.name: Final Project Modes, Teams, and Value Add
unit.description: Shifts from neural-network content to course project policy, covering collaboration rules, expectations for different team sizes, and the choice between the default and custom final projects.
unit.summary: The lecturer transitions from model content to the course final project and emphasizes that using outside code is allowed, but students must document what they used and demonstrate their own value add rather than merely running an existing model [ts=2270s] [ts=2320s]. He frames the project choice as default versus custom, encourages teams of one to three, and explains that larger teams or multi-course/shared efforts are expected to produce proportionally more work [ts=2349s] [ts=2535s]. He also recommends talking with TAs about topic fit and explains the default project: complete and experiment with a minimal BERT implementation for sentiment analysis, then extend it in an open-ended way, for example with paraphrasing, contrastive learning, or LoRA-style adaptation [ts=2606s] [ts=2664s]. The segment contrasts that guided path with custom projects for students who already have a research idea or want the experience of scoping data, methods, and evaluation from scratch [ts=2672s] [ts=2759s].

key_points.text: Using existing code is allowed, but students must document it and contribute clear value add.
Larger teams are expected to deliver proportionally more work than smaller teams.
Shared projects across multiple classes are allowed, but the expected workload scales up accordingly.
The default project centers on finishing and experimenting with a minimal BERT implementation for sentiment analysis.
Custom projects are appropriate when students have a topic they are genuinely excited to investigate.

He also recommends talking with TAs about topic fit and explains the default project: complete and experiment with a minimal BERT implementation for sentiment analysis, then extend it in an open-ended way, for example with paraphrasing, contrastive learning, or LoRA-style adaptation [ts=2606s] [ts=2664s]. The segment contrasts that guided path with custom projects for students who already have a research idea or want the experience of scoping data, methods, and evaluation from scratch [ts=2672s] [ts=2759s].

kp_catalog.name: Default versus custom final project choice
kp_catalog.description: Students may either complete the guided default BERT-based project or define a custom project, with expectations calibrated by team size and scope.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because default versus custom final project choice clarifies students may either complete the guided default BERT-based project or define a custom project, with expectations calibrated by team size and scope, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Value add, baselines, and evaluation design
kp_catalog.description: Projects are judged by what new insight, comparison, or improvement they contribute, so they need baselines, measurable evaluation, and analysis rather than just model usage.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because value add, baselines, and evaluation design formalizes how projects are judged by what new insight, comparison, or improvement they contribute, so they need baselines, measurable evaluation, and analysis rather than just model usage, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
